# ANDURIL-PRESS — Design Direction for AtomEons

## The thesis

Defense contractors and intelligence agencies don't build websites that look like SaaS. They build sites that look like annual reports printed on heavy stock and photographed for *The Economist*. The page scrolls slowly. The photography is large and deliberate. The accent color appears three times per screen, not thirty. The prose is restrained because the gravity is in the substance.

AtomEons is a cybersecurity lab, an alternative university for AI, and a design-inspiration target. ANDURIL-PRESS hits all three by refusing to play any of them as a category. We are not pitching defense. We are the institution doing the work. We are not advertising school. We are publishing the curriculum as a public record. We are not chasing Awwwards. We are the thing Awwwards chases.

## Palette

- `bg`: `#0A0E14` (ink — not pure black, gunmetal-with-warmth, holds detail at depth)
- `fg`: `#E8EAED` (paper white, 1.4% warmer than #FFFFFF, reduces screen burn)
- `border`: `#1F2630` (graphite line, 6% lift from bg, draws without shouting)
- `muted`: `#7A8290` (drafting-table gray for secondary text and metadata)
- `accent`: `#FF4D1A` (Anduril-aware orange-red, single deep accent, used only on live state, primary CTA, and the signature move)
- `warn`: `#FFB300` (sodium-vapor amber, advisory only, never decorative)

The current bio-cyan #22F0D5 retires. It reads SaaS-medical. The amber #FFB87A retires too — too close to the new warn channel. One accent, used sparingly, beats a duo used everywhere. Real institutions don't have brand gradients.

## Typography

- **Heading**: Söhne Breit (or fallback Inter Display tight-tracked at -0.02em). Heavy weight 600 on display, 500 on H2/H3. Editorial proportions, not UI.
- **Body**: Inter Variable, already loaded as `--font-inter`. Weight 400 at 17px desktop / 16px mobile. Line-height 1.6. Measure capped at 68ch.
- **Mono**: Berkeley Mono (paid) or fallback JetBrains Mono. Used for receipts, hashes, paper IDs, timestamps, version stamps. The mono channel is what makes the site read as a *laboratory*, not a startup.
- **Scale rule**: 1.250 minor-third on desktop, compressed to 1.200 on mobile. Display 64/80/96. Body fixed. No fluid type below 17px — readability is doctrine.

## Hero pattern

Above the fold: one black-and-white photograph that fills 100vw × 80vh — a stack of servers in cold light, or a hand on a thermal-camera reticle, or sodium-lit razor wire against fog. Editorial photography, not stock, not 3D, not gradient mesh. Top-left over the photo: small mono-stamp `ATOMEONS SYSTEMS LABORATORY · MARCO ISLAND FL · EST. 2025`. Centered low: one line of display-weight headline ("WE STUDY THE THINGS THAT BREAK"). Bottom-right: a single `#FF4D1A` dot, 8px, blinking 1Hz — the live-state marker. No subhead. No CTA above the fold. Scroll-cue: a mono line `01 / 07 — DOCTRINE`.

## Signature move

**The Live Receipts Strip.** A persistent 32px bar pinned to the bottom of every page that streams real telemetry in mono: latest paper SHA, last commit hash, build timestamp, current bench number, currently-running gate. Updates every 4 seconds via SSE. No animation other than the digit-flip on update. This is the thing nobody else has and nobody else can fake — because it requires actually doing the work. It tells cybersecurity people we're real, tells learners the lab is alive, tells designers we have a signature that requires substance.

## Motion language

Slow. 600ms eases on section reveals. No parallax. No spring physics. Mono digits flip with a 80ms tick. The accent dot pulses on a 1Hz mechanical heartbeat. Cursor leaves a single 1px guide-line when hovering over receipt rows. Reduced-motion users get static everything, lose nothing.

## Navigation

Header: thin 56px bar, `ATOMEONS` wordmark left in display weight, four items right — `LAB`, `LEARN`, `PAPERS`, `BUILDS`. Active state is a 1px `#FF4D1A` underline at 2px offset. No dropdowns. No hamburger on desktop. Mobile collapses to a `+` that expands a full-screen monochrome panel with the four items at display scale.

## Footer

Three-column editorial. Left: full mailing address, ORCID-style researcher line, public PGP fingerprint. Center: paper index (latest 5 SHA-stamped titles). Right: build telemetry mirror of the live receipts strip. Bottom rule: `ATOMEONS SYSTEMS LABORATORY · ATOM MCCREE · NO INVESTORS · NO BOARD · NO INFLUENCERS`.

## Photography direction (Nano Banana Pro prompt template)

```
Editorial black-and-white photograph, 35mm grain, deep shadows holding
detail, single hard light source from upper-left, subject [SUBJECT] in
industrial laboratory setting, no people unless silhouetted, no logos,
no text, composition rule-of-thirds with negative space upper-right,
mood — Anduril press kit meets Magnum Photos, shot on Leica M11
Monochrom equivalent, full tonal range from #0A0E14 to #E8EAED, zero
saturation, zero color cast, 16:10 frame.
```

Apply to server racks, oscilloscopes, GPU dies, whiteboard equations, satellite dishes at dusk, hands on keyboards in low light.

## Homepage outline

1. **Hero** — single photograph + display headline + receipts dot
2. **Doctrine strip** — four short paragraphs at display scale, one per section, scroll-locked
3. **Active papers** — latest 3 ÆoNs papers as full-bleed cards with mono SHA stamps
4. **Curriculum** — public AI college, 12 disciplines as a grid of mono labels, no marketing copy
5. **Cyber lab** — live vulnerability classes we're tracking, with paper links
6. **Builds** — OrangeBox and B00KMakor as editorial product plates, not store tiles
7. **Receipts wall** — last 30 days of build telemetry as a scrollable mono table
8. **Footer** — see above

## Why this wins

**Cybersecurity**: the visual register matches Anduril and Palantir, who are the people defense buyers already trust. We don't need to look like a SOC dashboard — we need to look like the people who supply the SOC.

**AI college**: published curriculum at display scale with real paper SHAs makes us look like a university press, not a course platform. Coursera looks like a marketplace. We look like Cambridge.

**Design inspiration**: the Live Receipts Strip is unfakeable. It enters Awwwards on substance, not animation tricks.

## Honest concessions

The Live Receipts Strip requires real backend plumbing we don't yet have — an SSE endpoint that exposes build/paper/gate telemetry. Two-week build minimum.

Single deep accent means we lose the dual-color memorability of the current bio-cyan/amber pair. The trade is gravity over vibrance.

Editorial photography costs real money or real shot time. Nano Banana Pro gets us 80% there; the remaining 20% is human curation, which I should not pretend away.

Söhne Breit is paid. Inter Display tight-tracked is the honest fallback until license is approved.

# WARM-ACADEMIC: Cream Paper, Deep Navy, the AtomEons Research Quarterly

## The premise

Burn the dark-mode-only constraint. The internet's serious work — Anthropic's research notes, Stripe's Press, Ben Evans' essays, the LRB, Edward Tufte's books, the original Bell Labs Technical Journal — lives on light paper. Dark mode is for tools you stare at; light mode is for things you're meant to read, keep, and cite. AtomEons should look like a working research laboratory that happens to ship products, not a cybersecurity startup that happens to publish. Cream paper. Navy ink. A serif that knows what it's doing. This is what a $1T research company looks like when it stops auditioning for Y Combinator.

## Palette

- **Background (paper):** `#F4EFE6` — warm cream, not gallery white. About 8% warmer than Anthropic's `#F8F4EE`. Reads as paper under tungsten, not screen under fluorescents.
- **Background secondary (card / sunken):** `#EBE4D4` — same hue, one stop darker for inset panels and code blocks.
- **Foreground (ink):** `#0E1A2B` — deep navy, not black. Black on cream reads cheap; navy reads bound-volume. This is the LRB move.
- **Foreground muted:** `#4A5468` — for metadata, captions, footnotes.
- **Border:** `#D8CFBC` — visible-but-quiet hairline. 1px is enough.
- **Accent (primary):** `#B5371A` — burnt sienna / oxide red. The color of red wax seals, Penguin Classics spines, danger annotations in old engineering manuals. Used for links, the live counter, and exactly one CTA.
- **Accent (secondary / cyber):** `#1E4D3B` — deep forest green. For status pills, "verified", "live", and the cybersecurity surface. Reads as institutional, not gamer.
- **Warn:** `#A8721A` — antique gold. For "preview", "beta", "draft" labels. Never bright orange.
- **Citation rule:** `#8A7E66` — for footnote dividers and the under-line of every cited claim.

No gradients. No glassmorphism. No glow. The shadow language is `0 1px 0 #D8CFBC` — a single hairline rule, the way books are bound.

## Typography

- **Display (headings):** **Söhne Breit** if budget allows, else **GT Sectra Display** — a contemporary serif with a true italic. Headings set in roman for institutional pages, italic for essays and field notes. Sectra has the knife-edge that reads as expensive without screaming.
- **Body:** **Source Serif 4** or **Tiempos Text** — old-style figures on by default. Tracking `-0.005em` at body size. This is the font of long-form reading, not interface chrome.
- **UI / metadata:** **Inter Variable** (already loaded as `--font-inter`) — only for nav, buttons, form fields, footer columns. Never for hero copy.
- **Mono:** **JetBrains Mono** at 0.92x for inline code, hashes, ledger rows, version stamps.
- **Scale:** modular, 1.25 ratio. `13 / 16 / 20 / 25 / 31 / 39 / 49 / 61 / 76 / 95`. Hero display sets at 95px on desktop, 49px on mobile. Body at 20px (the reading-app standard, not the website standard). Line-height 1.55 on body, 1.05 on display.
- **Rule:** every page has exactly one display-weight heading. The rest is hierarchy through size and italics, not weight.

## Hero pattern

Above the fold, three things only.

1. **Top-left masthead block:** `THE ATOMEONS RESEARCH QUARTERLY · VOL. III · MARCO ISLAND, FLORIDA · ISSUE №47` set in 13px Inter caps, letterspaced `0.12em`, navy on cream. A hairline rule beneath.
2. **Center column, 720px max:** a serif italic headline — "*A laboratory for things that aren't supposed to exist yet.*" — at 76–95px, navy ink, roman drop-cap on the first letter rendered as an actual illuminated initial in burnt sienna. Below it: a single body paragraph in 20px Source Serif, 8–10 lines, stating the thesis in lab-grade prose. Beneath the paragraph: one CTA — "Read the current issue →" in burnt sienna, underlined on hover with a hand-drawn waving rule, not a CSS underline.
3. **Right gutter (desktop only):** the live "in this issue" column — current paper, current build, current cyber advisory, current Founder's View letter. Each one a single-line entry with date, title, and a 1px rule between. This is where the build-in-public counter lives, restyled as `STRIPE LIVE · $X,XXX · UPDATED 14:32 EDT`.

No hero image above the fold. The image lives below it, as the **plate** — a single full-bleed Nano Banana Pro photograph with a roman-numeral caption.

## The signature move

**The Marginalia.** Every claim on the site — every number, every product callout, every roadmap item — has a footnote anchor. Click it (or hover on desktop), and the entire right margin of the page expands into a hand-set marginal note with the source, the date, the method, and a permalink to the receipt in the Ledger. The footnote isn't a tooltip. It's a typeset margin gloss with its own typography, its own rule, its own hierarchy — the way Tufte does it in *Visual Display* or the way the original Principia annotates its propositions. **No competitor has this.** It collapses cyber-credibility (every claim sourced), college-credibility (footnoted like a journal), and design-inspiration (a visible typographic move you've never seen on a SaaS site) into one gesture. People will screenshot it. That's the goal.

## Motion language

Reluctant. Print doesn't move. Motion appears in exactly four places: (1) the marginalia expansion, which slides in with a 220ms ease-out and a hairline rule that draws itself left-to-right at 180ms; (2) the masthead's issue-number ticker, which crossfades on Stripe webhook receipts; (3) link underlines, which draw on hover at 140ms; (4) the page-turn transition between long-form essays, which uses a 320ms paper-fold easing on the body content only. No parallax. No scroll-jacking. No hero video. Reduced-motion users get zero motion and lose nothing.

## Navigation

A single horizontal rule across the top, no background fill. Left: `ATOMEONS` in 16px Inter caps, letterspaced `0.16em`. Center: five items — `Research · Build · Cyber · Learn · Ledger`. Right: a single account dot and a search glyph. Active state is a 2px burnt-sienna underline on the current section, drawn the same way as link hovers. On scroll, the rule tightens to 56px and the masthead disappears; nothing else changes. Mobile: the five items collapse into a serif italic word — *Index* — which opens a full-page table of contents set in display serif, not a hamburger drawer.

## Footer

Three columns, hairline rules, no logos. Left column: **Colophon** — typeset in Florida, set in Source Serif 4 and Inter, served from Vercel, last revised [date]. Center: **Sections** — five links matching nav. Right: **Subscribe** — one email field, label "Receive the Quarterly by post (digital)." Beneath the columns, a single roman-numeral page number (`p. I`) and the SHA-256 of the current build. That's it. No social icons. No newsletter pitch. No "© 2026 All Rights Reserved." The footer reads as a book's colophon page, because that's what it is.

## Photography direction (Nano Banana Pro)

Every plate is a single black-and-white photograph with deep grain, shot as if from a 1960s research-laboratory archive. Tungsten lighting, single source, deep shadows. No people unless they are working, hands visible, faces partial. No stock-photo affect.

**Prompt template:**
> *Black and white archival photograph, medium format Tri-X 400 film grain, single tungsten key light from upper left, deep shadow on right. Subject: [SUBJECT — e.g., a single open laboratory notebook on a wooden desk with a fountain pen and one ceramic cup of coffee, or hands at a keyboard with a CRT monitor showing terminal text, or the corner of a server rack with one amber LED]. Composition: rule-of-thirds, negative space upper right for caption overlay. Mood: serious, working, lived-in, mid-task. Texture: visible paper grain, real focus falloff, no digital sharpening. No text, no logos, no stock-photo gloss. Late afternoon. Marco Island, Florida. The room of someone who builds things that matter.*

Every plate carries a **roman-numeral caption** beneath it in 13px Inter caps — `PLATE XIV · A SINGLE LINE OF PYTHON, REWRITTEN ELEVEN TIMES.` This is the visual signature of the site. People will know an AtomEons image at a glance.

## Homepage outline

1. **The Masthead + Hero** — issue number, serif italic thesis, single CTA, live column.
2. **The Plate** — full-bleed B&W photograph with roman-numeral caption and a one-paragraph editor's note in italics.
3. **The Current Issue** — a typeset table-of-contents listing the current Founder's View letter, the current research paper, the current cyber advisory, the current Build receipt. Each row is a serif italic title, an Inter-caps section label, a date, and a 1px rule. Looks like the contents page of a journal.
4. **The Ledger Strip** — a single horizontal band, hairline rules above and below, showing the live Stripe counter ("$X,XXX through Vol. III"), the install count, the last commit SHA, and the last cyber advisory ID. Cream background, navy text, no decoration. This is where cybersecurity gravitas + build-in-public truth meet.
5. **The Three Faculties** — three columns: **Research** (papers, with the current top three listed in italic), **Build** (products: OrangeBox, B00KMakor, Skilskis — each with a single black-and-white plate and a one-line gloss), **Learn** (the college alternative — curricula listed as if they were graduate courses, "CYB 401: Adversarial Cryptography from First Principles"). No buttons. Each column ends with "→ enter the [Faculty]" as an italic link.
6. **A Long Footnote** — a single long-form paragraph, set indented and italic, explaining what AtomEons is in the voice of the operator. Not marketing copy. The kind of paragraph that appears as the introduction to a book of essays. Signed `— Atom McCree, Marco Island, [date]` in handwritten-feeling serif.
7. **The Subscription Card** — a typeset card, hairline border, offering the Quarterly by email. One field, one button. Below it, the masthead "since 2024 · vol. III · issue 47."
8. **The Colophon (footer)** — three-column, hairline-ruled, as described above.

Eight sections. None of them are decorative. Each one earns its place by Mom's Law.

## Why this wins on all three identities

**Cybersecurity leader:** Nobody in the cyber space looks like this. CrowdStrike, Palo Alto, SentinelOne, Wiz, Snyk — they all run the same dark navy + electric accent + glass-shard hero. A cream-paper, serif-italic, footnoted-marginalia site reads as *graver* than any of them — it reads like the kind of laboratory that actually writes the CVEs the others read. Cyber gravitas does not come from neon green; it comes from looking like you've been doing this since 1962. Bell Labs. Lockheed Skunk Works. NSA's *Cryptologic Quarterly*. That's the lineage we claim.

**AI college alternative:** This is what a real research university's deepest pages look like — Princeton IAS, the Santa Fe Institute, the MIT Press catalog. Every other "AI college" looks like a Substack or a Notion template. WARM-ACADEMIC visually claims peer-rank with the institutions students would otherwise pay $80K/year to attend. The marginalia + footnotes make every page feel like a graduate seminar. Free education doesn't have to look free.

**Design inspiration:** Every Awwwards-tier site of the last three years has been dark, gradient, kinetic, and forgettable. A cream-paper site with hand-typeset margin notes is genuinely uncommon — the only peers are personal sites by Tobias van Schneider, Frank Chimero, and Rauno's experiments. As a *product* site at this typographic level, there are essentially none. Designers will share it the day it ships. That is the cheapest distribution we have.

## Honest concessions

1. **The serif-display dependency.** Source Serif 4 is free; Tiempos and GT Sectra are not. A licensed face costs ~$600–1,200. If we ship on Source Serif + an Adobe Fonts pairing, we lose 10% of the knife-edge. Acceptable; we can upgrade in Vol. IV.
2. **Light-mode-only.** Some users genuinely prefer dark mode and will bounce. The bet is that the audience this site is built to attract (researchers, design-leads, senior engineers, CISO-tier buyers) reads on light by preference and prints to paper regularly. We accept the loss of the dark-mode-default cohort. We can ship a `[ ] Lights out` toggle in the footer that inverts to a faithful dark-on-dark mode in Vol. IV.
3. **Marginalia on mobile.** The right-margin gloss does not exist on phones. Footnotes collapse to bottom-of-section endnotes there. The signature move is desktop-first. Acceptable; the audience for the signature move reads on laptops.
4. **Black-and-white photography is harder than color.** Nano Banana Pro will need careful prompting and a curated library before ship. Plan: a 12-image plate library for V1, expanded to 40 by Vol. IV. We accept reshoots.
5. **No motion-graphic hero.** We forfeit the WebGL-bait that wins certain Awwwards juries. We win a different jury — the one that ranks Tufte over Bruno Simon. Acceptable; that jury also writes the checks.
6. **Serif italics at 95px are unforgiving.** Bad kerning will be visible. Every display headline gets a manual pass before ship. Mom's Law applies.
7. **The cybersecurity audience may initially read "academic" as "soft."** Mitigated by the Ledger Strip and the cyber-advisory ID being prominent on the homepage — institutional gravitas, not academic remove. We carry receipts where it counts.

This is the site. Cream paper, navy ink, burnt-sienna seal, marginalia in the margins, plates beneath the fold, the SHA-256 in the colophon. The Quarterly, Vol. III.

# OPERATOR-TERMINAL — Design Direction

A live mission console, not a homepage. The site behaves like a station you're cleared into: every surface reports something real, every panel has a callsign, every claim is sourced inline. Bloomberg's information density, the New York Times' editorial restraint, Anduril's defense-grade gravitas, and Linear's geometric precision — fused into a monospace-first cybersecurity-and-academic command center that an SOC analyst, a PhD candidate, and a design lead all recognize as theirs.

**Palette.** Background `#070A0F` (deep slate, near-black with the faintest cyan undertone — never pure #000, which reads cheap). Surface layer `#0E141C` for cards and panels. Foreground `#E6EDF3` (off-white, paper not snow). Muted text `#7D8B9C`. Border `#1A2330` (load-bearing — every panel gets a 1px line, no shadows, no rounded-corner softness). Hairline border `#0F1620` for nested separators. Accent cyan `#22F0D5` — keep this, it's already brand equity, and on slate it stops feeling decorative and starts feeling like a CRT phosphor. Warn amber `#FFB87A` retained but downgraded to "advisory" — used only for live alerts and timestamps. Add critical red `#FF4D5A` for one purpose: breach indicators and shipped-to-prod commit dots. Pass green `#3DDC97` for receipt-verified states. That's it. Six tokens. No gradients except one — a 1px-tall cyan-to-transparent line under the hero as scan signature.

**Typography.** Heading: **Berkeley Mono** (or **JetBrains Mono** as the free fallback) at display weights — yes, monospace headlines. This is the unlock. Body: **Inter Variable** at 15px/24px with `font-feature-settings: "ss01", "cv11", "tnum"` for slashed-zero and tabular numerals. Mono inline: same Berkeley/JetBrains. Scale rule: a strict 4-step ramp — 12px (meta), 15px (body), 21px (subhead), 36px (section), 64px (hero). No 18px, no 28px, no in-betweens. Every text element snaps to the grid. Tracking on display monospace: `-0.02em`. Line-height: 1.15 on display, 1.6 on body.

**Hero pattern.** Above the fold splits into a three-zone command panel. Left third (40%): the operator wordmark `ATOMEONS // SYSTEMS LABORATORY`, a single 64px monospace claim ("Defense-grade AI, taught in the open."), and a two-button CTA row labeled `[ENTER TERMINAL]` and `[REQUEST CLEARANCE]` styled as bracketed terminal prompts. Right two-thirds: a live 4-tile data grid showing `THREAT FEED` (rotating real CVE IDs from NVD with timestamps), `CURRICULUM STATUS` (modules shipped, with green dots), `BUILD LEDGER` (last 5 commit SHAs with SHA-256 stamps), and `OPERATOR LOG` (Atom's last 3 declassified entries, each with a timestamp and location stamp — `MARCO ISLAND // 14:23 EST`). Each tile has a callsign in its corner: `TF-01`, `CR-02`, `BL-03`, `OL-04`. Behind everything: a 32-column dot grid at 4% opacity. No hero image. The data IS the hero.

**Signature move.** **The Receipt Tape.** A horizontal scrolling ticker fixed to the bottom of the viewport, 28px tall, monospace, that streams real cryptographic receipts in real time: SHA-256 hashes of shipped work, commit timestamps, paper disclosure IDs (`ATOM-CLC-2026-0331`), threat indicators, paid invoice confirmations. Click any receipt and a panel slides up showing the underlying artifact. This is the thing nobody else has — not Anduril, not Anthropic, not Stripe. It says "everything on this site is sourced and verifiable" without ever saying it.

**Motion language.** Almost none, deployed surgically. The Receipt Tape scrolls continuously at 40px/sec — the only persistent motion. Tile values count up on first view with `tabular-nums` ticking for 600ms then locking. Section transitions are instant — no fades, no parallax, no scroll-jacking. Hovers: a 1px border flash to cyan over 80ms, that's it. The cursor over interactive elements becomes a 12px monospace bracket reticle. Page navigation: a 200ms cyan scan-line wipes top-to-bottom. Motion exists to confirm input, never to perform.

**Navigation.** Header is a 56px-tall monospace bar: `ATOMEONS//SYSLAB` left, then five tokens `[INTEL] [ACADEMY] [PRODUCTS] [PAPERS] [OPERATOR]`. Active state: the bracket becomes solid cyan and a 2px cyan underline appears. Right side: a live timestamp in the operator's timezone, and `[STATUS: OPERATIONAL]` with a green dot. No hamburger on mobile — the nav collapses to a full-screen terminal overlay with the same five tokens stacked.

**Footer.** A full-width briefing card. Four columns of monospace links under callsigns `TRANSMIT`, `DOSSIERS`, `LICENSING`, `DECLASS`. Beneath: a 200-character mission statement, the lab's PGP fingerprint, a real coordinate string `25.9407°N 81.7187°W`, and the build hash of the deployed site. No social icons — links are plain text. No newsletter form — it's `[REQUEST CLEARANCE]` again.

**Photography direction.** Nano Banana Pro prompt template: *"Defense-industrial command center photography, shot on Phase One IQ4, 35mm, available light only, no flash. Subject: [equipment / interior / operator hands at terminal]. Mood: pre-dawn or 3am, monitors glowing cyan-#22F0D5, ambient slate-#070A0F walls, single warm tungsten source for skin. Aesthetic reference: Trent Parke meets Anduril press kit meets Christopher Doyle. Grain: ISO 800 film. Composition: rule-of-thirds, generous negative space top-right for overlay text. No lens flare, no chromatic aberration, no Instagram filter. Documentary not editorial. Lab-grade not cinematic."* All hero imagery: people from behind or hands only — never faces. Faces personalize; we're a system.

**Homepage outline.**
1. **Hero command panel** (three-zone, described above).
2. **The Three Identities** — three monospace columns side-by-side: `CYBERSECURITY` / `ACADEMY` / `LABORATORY`, each with one 21px sentence, a stat block (`47 ACTIVE CVES TRACKED`, `212 MODULES SHIPPED`, `19 PAPERS PUBLISHED`), and a single link.
3. **Live Threat Feed** — full-width data table, sortable, real CVE pull, with cyan severity dots. Every row clickable for the dossier.
4. **Academy: The Curriculum** — a 4×3 grid of course cards, each shown as a redacted dossier folder — callsign, runtime, difficulty bar in mono characters (`[▓▓▓▓░░░░]`), `[OPEN COURSE]` link. Free, stated plainly.
5. **Operator Log** — Atom's three most recent founder's-view letters, shown as terminal output with `>` prompts.
6. **Receipts Wall** — a 6×4 grid of SHA-256 stamps from real shipped work, each hoverable to reveal the artifact. The Receipt Tape's still archive.
7. **Products** — OrangeBox and B00KMakor as two large dossier cards, no marketing copy, just specs and `[ACQUIRE]`.
8. **Briefing Footer** (described above).

**Why this wins on three fronts.** Cybersecurity: monospace + live threat feed + cryptographic receipts is the native dialect of SOC analysts and red-teamers — they'll trust it instantly because it looks like their tools, not like a SaaS landing page. AI college alternative: the dossier-card curriculum and terminal-output operator log read as graduate-seminar serious — not Khan Academy bright, not Coursera corporate, something closer to MIT OpenCourseWare crossed with a declassified DARPA brief. Design inspiration: nobody is doing monospace-display-headlines with a persistent verifiable-receipt ticker. Awwwards juries reward the *unrepeatable signature move* — the Receipt Tape is that move. It's also the rare signature that's functional, not decorative, which is what separates a Site of the Year from a Site of the Day.

**Honest concessions.** Monospace display type fatigues at long-form reading — we hard-switch to Inter the moment we leave headers, no exceptions. The Receipt Tape will cost engineering: it needs a real receipt-emit pipeline behind it, not fake hashes, or the whole thesis collapses on first inspection. The palette is narrow — designers used to gradient richness will call it austere; that's the point, but it does mean we live or die on typography and information density, not on color delight. Mobile loses the four-tile hero grid and collapses to a single-column stream, which is less impressive than the desktop reveal. And finally: this direction is opinionated against the current bio-cyan-on-black warmth — the warmth gets traded for gravitas, and that's a real trade. If the operator wants the site to feel inviting, this isn't it. This site feels *cleared*.

# FINTECH-CLEAN Direction — AtomEons Homepage Redesign

## The thesis

Mercury made banking look like infrastructure software. Plaid made financial plumbing feel auditable. AtomEons makes cybersecurity defense and AI education look like the same kind of institutional certainty — calm, white, dense with verifiable evidence. The current black + bio-cyan is theater for a company that doesn't need theater anymore. Burn it. We're not a launch anymore. We're a laboratory with revenue, 200+ pages, and a brief to outclass a $1T cohort. Black sites look like everyone. White sites with deliberate color blocks look like the institutions that audit the black sites.

## Palette

**Foundation (95% of surface):**
- `--bg` `#FAFAF7` — warm off-white, photographer's neutral, not Apple-cold
- `--surface` `#FFFFFF` — pure white, used only for elevated cards and hero
- `--fg` `#0A0A0B` — near-black with the faintest blue cast, never `#000`
- `--fg-muted` `#5A5D63` — body secondary
- `--border` `#E8E6E0` — warm hairline, 1px everywhere, never gray

**Color blocks (one per section, never two adjacent):**
- `--cyber` `#0B3D2E` — dark forest green, the cybersecurity block (Anduril gravitas without copying their orange)
- `--learn` `#1B2A4E` — Prussian navy, the AI-college block (Stanford-press, not Yale-stuffy)
- `--design` `#F4EBD9` — bone cream, the design-inspiration block (Awwwards-respectable)
- `--accent` `#FF5B1F` — single chromatic accent, used on exactly three CTAs site-wide

**Functional:**
- `--warn` `#B45309` — burnt amber for security receipts and live status
- `--ok` `#0E7A4F` — green for verified/signed
- `--code-bg` `#0A0A0B` — mono blocks invert to dark; the only place black survives

This palette wins because each of the three identities gets its own quadrant — forest = defense, navy = academy, cream = gallery — and they share the same warm-white substrate so the site reads as one organism, not three sites stapled together.

## Typography

- **Display heading:** **Söhne Breit** (or self-hosted **Pangea Afrikan** if Söhne is out of budget — both have the Mercury-grade neogrotesque feel with slightly wider counters than Inter). Set in two weights only: 500 and 700.
- **Body:** Keep **Inter Variable** — it's already loaded, it's optical-sized, and it pairs cleanly with a wider display face. Weights 400 and 500 only.
- **Mono:** **JetBrains Mono Variable** for receipts, hashes, code blocks, and any time we're showing primary-source evidence. This is the cybersecurity signal.

**Scale rule:** Modular scale at 1.25 ratio anchored at 16px body. Display steps: 16 → 20 → 24 → 32 → 48 → 64 → 96 → 128. Never use a size between steps. Hero gets exactly one 128px line, nothing else on the page touches it.

## Hero pattern

Above the fold, full viewport, three zones stacked:

**Top bar (60px):** Live receipt strip — small mono text scrolling left, "SIG-VERIFIED · ATOM-AESUITE-2026-0419 · 230/230 PEER REVIEW · LATTICE SHA c8a4e9…" — actual rotating hashes from the ledger. This is the cybersecurity tell visible in the first 200ms.

**Center (calc(100vh - 240px)):** Single line, left-aligned, 128px display: **"A laboratory, not a launch."** Below it, one 24px line in `--fg-muted`: "Cybersecurity defense, masters-grade AI education, and the open-notebook research feed of one independent lab. Marco Island, Florida." Below that, two CTAs side by side — primary in `--accent` orange ("Read the receipts"), secondary as text-only with underline ("Audit the code").

**Bottom bar (180px):** Three equal columns, hairline-divided, each showing one identity quadrant — a single mono number, a label, a forest/navy/cream color dot. Example: `2,847` ATTACK VECTORS DOCUMENTED · `721` SKILLS PUBLISHED FREE · `34` PEER-REVIEWED PAPERS. Numbers tick live from real data, not loops.

No hero image, no gradient, no animated mesh. White ground, black type, three color dots. The composition is the signature.

## Signature move

**The Ledger Strip.** A persistent 32px horizontal band that lives just below the global header on every page. It's a live, scrolling, mono-typeface feed of every cryptographic receipt the lab has issued today — SHA-256 stamps, paper IDs, security audit pass tokens, package ledger rows. Click any entry, it expands to show the full receipt inline. Hover, it pauses. This is the thing nobody else does: a public, real-time, primary-source proof feed that proves we're a lab and not a marketing site. It satisfies cybersecurity (verifiable signatures), AI-college (open research notebook), and design-inspiration (nothing on the web looks like a Bloomberg terminal married to a museum wall label) in one component.

## Motion language

Three rules:
1. **Functional motion only.** Hover states 120ms ease-out. Page transitions 180ms cross-fade on the main content region — the header and ledger strip never move.
2. **The Ledger Strip scrolls at 18px/sec.** Linear, never paused unless hovered. It is the heartbeat.
3. **No parallax, no scroll-jacking, no Lottie, no GSAP-pinned anything.** Stripe-grade restraint. The page does what the user asks it to do.

## Navigation

Sticky header, 64px, white surface, hairline bottom border. Wordmark left in 20px display weight 700. Five items right-aligned in 14px Inter 500: **Defend · Learn · Build · Papers · About**. Active state is a 2px `--accent` underline that animates in from left, 200ms. No dropdowns. Each item is a real page, not a mega-menu theater. Mobile: hamburger opens full-screen white sheet with the same five items at 48px display.

## Footer

Three-column on desktop, single column on mobile. Left: wordmark, address (Marco Island, FL), the operator's name and email. Center: site map in four short columns. Right: the current Suite SHA-256 hash in mono, and a "Subscribe to the lab notebook" single-field email input (Loops). Bottom hairline, then one line: "AtomEons Systems Laboratory · ÆoNs Research · 2026 · No tracking, no analytics, no dark patterns. Source available." That sentence is the entire trust posture.

## Photography direction (Nano Banana Pro prompts)

Template — paste-fill the brackets:

> "Editorial documentary photograph, [SUBJECT: e.g. a hands-on-keyboard shot of a security researcher's workstation], natural north-facing window light, slight cool cast in shadows, warm cast in highlights, shot on Hasselblad H6D-100c with 80mm Hasselblad XCD lens, f/4.0, ISO 200, no flash, no lens flare. Composition: rule-of-thirds, subject in left two-thirds, negative space right one-third for type overlay. Color grade: muted, desaturated reds, retained greens and navies, warm off-white walls #FAFAF7, no pure blacks. Surface materials visible: brushed aluminum, raw oak, matte black anodized hardware, single soft houseplant. No people's faces in frame; hands and shoulders only. No stock-photo gloss, no LED RGB. Mood: a working laboratory at 7am on a Tuesday."

Three image families: Cyber (forest-tinged, hardware-forward), Learn (navy-tinged, paper-and-screen), Design (cream-tinged, materials-and-light). 67 existing images stay; new images render to this prompt.

## Homepage outline (7 sections)

1. **Hero** — as specified above. White, single 128px line, live receipts top, three numbers bottom.
2. **The Three Identities Block** — three full-bleed color panels stacked vertically (forest / navy / cream), each holds one identity statement at 64px, one paragraph of evidence in 18px Inter, and one CTA. Forest panel = Defend (Cyber). Navy = Learn (AI college). Cream = Inspire (Design).
3. **The Ledger, exploded** — full-width white section showing the Ledger Strip blown up to a 600px panel — readable, sortable, filterable. "Every claim on this site has a receipt. Browse them."
4. **Free Masters-Grade Curriculum** — a 12-column grid showing the 721 skills as a dense terminal-style index, mono typeface, hover reveals title. The visual density itself is the proof of depth. "More course material than a $200K MS, indexed and signed."
5. **What Defends You Today** — three cards on cream substrate: OrangeBox (real product, real revenue), B00KMakor (real product, real revenue), the Suite (open source). Each card has a price, a SHA, a "buy" button. Commerce stays visible, not buried.
6. **The Lab Notebook** — six most recent papers/posts in a Wall-Street-Journal-style stacked-headline layout. Mono date, display headline, 2-line dek, mono author/disclosure-ID. Looks like a newspaper, not a blog.
7. **Closing Line + Footer** — one centered 96px sentence on warm-white: "Built by one person. Verified by mathematics." Then the footer.

## Why this wins on all three fronts

**Cybersecurity:** The Ledger Strip and the visible SHA-256 hashes are the loudest possible signal that this site treats cryptographic proof as decoration. No competitor in the space publishes their receipts on the homepage. Anduril shows missiles; we show signatures — different gravitas, same seriousness.

**AI college:** The 721-skill index in Section 4 is a wall of evidence. A prospective learner sees, in a single scroll, that the curriculum exceeds what they'd get for $200K elsewhere — and it's free, and it's signed. The dense terminal aesthetic also signals to the technical reader that we respect their time.

**Design inspiration:** The Ledger Strip is the Awwwards-eligible signature move — it's a component no other lab/security/edu site has shipped, it solves a real trust problem with a primary visual gesture, and it photographs beautifully for the design press. The restraint of the white ground + three-color-block system is what landed Mercury and Linear on Awwwards Site of the Year; we apply the same discipline to a different vertical.

## Honest concessions

- **Black + bio-cyan dies.** The current launch aesthetic gets retired. Old hero images in `/cyber-images/*` and `/learn-images/*` will look misaligned against a warm-white substrate; budget a re-shoot or a Nano Banana Pro re-grade pass to recolor them. Roughly 60% of existing images survive the transition without re-render.
- **The Ledger Strip is a real engineering build.** It needs a backing API that pulls from the actual ledger (zip-SHA rows), a websocket or polling cadence, and a "click to expand" component. Two-week build minimum for the v1, including the read-only public endpoint.
- **Söhne is licensed.** If the budget for a Söhne web-license is a no-go, fall back to **Inter Variable Display weights** at very tight tracking (-0.02em) — it's a B+ outcome, not A+. Pangea Afrikan is the open alternative if we want A+ for free.
- **No-tracking footer claim must be true.** If we're using Vercel Analytics or Loops conversion tracking, we either remove them or rephrase the claim. The footer sentence is load-bearing trust; we can't lie there.
- **Section 4 (721 skills index) renders 721 DOM nodes.** Needs virtualization (react-virtuoso or equivalent) on first paint or the homepage LCP suffers. Engineering ticket, not a design problem, but worth naming.
- **Orange `--accent` is reserved.** If the operator wants more chromatic punch elsewhere, that ambition gets routed into the next direction document — not this one. Restraint is the brand.
- **Voice register stays Atom, not Apple.** No "magical experiences," no "reimagined." Display lines are declarative, factual, lab-grade. "A laboratory, not a launch" is the upper bound of poetry allowed.

# LAB-NOTEBOOK Direction — AtomEons Systems Laboratory

The site reads like a published thesis from a national lab, not a startup landing page. Visitors should feel they have walked into MIT's Lincoln Laboratory crossed with a hand-bound research notebook — dense, sourced, technically literate, with the quiet confidence of an institution that has nothing to prove. Stripe taught the industry that financial infrastructure earns trust through typographic precision; we apply that lesson to cybersecurity and education. The aesthetic is "Anthropic's research page, but the entire site." It must beat Anduril on gravitas, beat MIT OpenCourseWare on academic depth, and beat Rauno on signature execution — simultaneously.

**Palette.** Burn the cyan-and-amber. Move to a paper-and-graphite system. Background `#F7F5EF` (warm cream, soft as Moleskine paper, not white). Primary foreground `#0C0C0E` (near-black graphite, never pure `#000`). Subtle border `#D9D4C8` (the color of aged thread between paper sheets). Single accent `#C84A2C` — a controlled vermilion, the color of a red sealing-wax stamp or a margin-note pen, used for indices, citations, equation numbers, and exactly one navigation state at a time. Warn `#7A5A1A` (oak gall ink, for security advisories). Dark-mode counterpart: bg `#0E0D0B`, fg `#EDE7D7`, border `#2A2723`, accent stays `#C84A2C`. Two colors plus one accent. That's the system. Anything else is decoration.

**Typography.** Headings: **Söhne Breit** or its open alternative **JetBrains Mono** at display weight — body in Inter (already loaded) at a tight 16/24 with `letter-spacing: -0.01em` and `font-feature-settings: "ss01", "cv11"`. Mono throughout the document for citations, equations, file paths, hashes, dates: **JetBrains Mono** or **IBM Plex Mono**. Scale follows a strict 1.250 (major third) ratio: 12 · 14 · 16 · 20 · 25 · 31 · 39 · 49 · 61. Headings are tight (`leading-[1.05]`), body is generous (`leading-[1.55]`), mono is condensed (`leading-[1.4]`). All headings use real small-caps via OpenType, not text-transform. Every section heading is preceded by a mono-rendered index number (`§ 03.2`) — this is the academic tell.

**Hero pattern.** Above the fold: a single full-bleed paper-textured field. Top-left: mono header reading `ATOMEONS SYSTEMS LABORATORY · MARCO ISLAND, FL · EST. 2026`. Center-left, a single thesis sentence in 61pt display: *"A research laboratory for cybersecurity, machine intelligence, and the systems they share."* No buttons in the hero. Below the headline, a 4-column dense info strip in 14pt mono — `ACTIVE PROGRAMS: 7` / `PUBLISHED PAPERS: 12` / `OPEN COURSES: 200+` / `COMPUTE HOURS LOGGED TODAY: 8,341`. The compute counter ticks in real time. Right side: a single oversized indexed marginalia card — handwritten-feeling annotation pointing to one current paper, with a red `§` glyph and a citation block. Below the fold, no carousel, no logos. The first scroll reveals §01.

**Signature move — The Live Lab Notebook.** The entire homepage is rendered as if it is one continuous unbound page of a real lab notebook. Every section has a hand-drawn rule line above it (SVG, not image — vector ink with slight imperfection). Every section is indexed (`§ 01 · INTAKE`, `§ 02 · CYBERSECURITY`, `§ 03 · LEARN`). At the right margin of every section, in a sticky rail, sits a live ticker of *real* compute happening on AtomEons systems — actual current load, last commit SHA, last paper SHA-256, last test pass count, time since last security advisory, GPU hours this week. This is the receipts wall, but architecturally embedded, not a section. **No competitor has this.** Anduril shows hardware photos. We show the lab breathing.

**Motion.** Motion is restrained to three behaviors. First, mono numbers tick on real data (compute counter, papers count) — Stripe-style monospace digit transitions, no rubber-band. Second, hand-drawn rules between sections draw themselves on scroll-into-view, single-stroke, 280ms ease-out. Third, hover on a section index reveals a quiet underline crawl in vermilion. No parallax, no scroll-jacking, no fade-ins, no Lottie. Reduced-motion users lose only the rule-drawing.

**Navigation.** Sticky header, transparent over cream, three items only: `Research` · `Courses` · `Systems`. Right side: `Sign in` in plain text, `Begin Intake →` in vermilion. Active state is a single vermilion `§` glyph next to the current item. No mega-menu. No hamburger on desktop. Mobile collapses to a single index sheet that reads like a table of contents.

**Footer.** Full-bleed cream. Left: full mailing address in mono, like a journal masthead. Center: a printed-style index of all site pages, organized as a true table of contents with page numbers (literally — `§ 04.2 · LIFEPATH ...... p.47`). Right: ISSN-style identifier line — `ATOMEONS-PRESS · VOL. 01 · ISSUE 06 · 2026-06-02`. A red wax-seal SVG bottom-right with `AE`.

**Photography direction (Nano Banana Pro prompt template).** `"Black-and-white archival photograph, 6x7 medium format film grain, single-light Rembrandt key on the left, [SUBJECT: research equipment / lab interior / single human hand annotating paper], shallow depth of field at f/2.8, paper-and-graphite tonality, no color, no logos, no text in image, no people-faces in frame, museum-archive composition, Hiroshi Sugimoto restraint, shot 2026, ungraded."` Every image lands as a duotone-printed plate with a mono caption beneath (`Plate 07 · OrangeBox compute envelope · 2026-05-14`). This single template covers cybersecurity (equipment), learning (hands and notation), and the design-inspiration crowd (Sugimoto-grade restraint).

**Homepage outline.** `§ 01 · Thesis` (hero). `§ 02 · The Three Programs` (Cybersecurity · Learn · Systems — three indexed paragraphs, one plate each, no cards). `§ 03 · Current Research` (the three most recent papers as bibliography entries with abstracts, mono citation block). `§ 04 · The Lab Notebook` (live receipts wall — compute, commits, advisories, SHA-256 of last shipped artifact). `§ 05 · Courses Available Now` (a literal course catalog page, indexed by department, mono prices in vermilion). `§ 06 · Open Compute` (OrangeBox + B00KMakor product entries written as appendix items, with technical specifications, not marketing copy). `§ 07 · Colophon` (typography, paper stock, build system, model versions used, hashes — the literal book colophon).

**Why this wins on all three.** Cybersecurity buyers want gravitas, citations, and signs of operational reality — a live receipts wall serving real compute hashes outperforms a fighter-jet photo for the audience that actually buys defense work. AI-college learners want academic depth — a homepage that *is* a table of contents tells them this is graduate-grade before they click. Awwwards judges want a memorable signature move — there is no other site on the web that renders itself as a real-time lab notebook with a vermilion vermarked margin. The aesthetic is unowned territory between Anthropic's restraint and a 1950s Bell Labs annual report.

**Honest concessions.** A cream-and-graphite site reads slower at first glance than a glowing cyber palette — the trade is depth for spectacle, and we accept it. Mono-heavy typography requires careful font loading or FOUT will look ugly on a paper background. The vermilion accent must be used with monastic discipline; one rogue red button breaks the whole effect. Reduced-color modes need testing — vermilion `#C84A2C` against cream is AA-compliant only at 16pt+ for body text. The 200+ existing pages will need a gradual retrofit — the Header/Footer/tokens-first approach is the right move, but expect three months before every interior page feels native.

# DESIGN-SYSTEM-MUSEUM — Direction for AtomEons.com

## The thesis

AtomEons becomes the first AI/cybersecurity company whose homepage is itself a published design system. Every visitor lands inside a living specimen book — swatches, type ramps, token tables, component galleries — that doubles as the marketing surface. We win design-inspiration credibility not by linking to a separate `/design` page, but by making the homepage the proof. We win cybersecurity gravitas through restraint: this is what a defense-grade interface looks like when nothing is decorative. We win the AI-college claim because museums teach.

Reference altitude: Vercel Geist docs · IBM Carbon design system site · Linear's brand pages · the Anthropic style guide · Stripe Press · Bruno Simon's portfolio sectioning · Pitch's marketing site · Figma's Config keynote pages. We are cinematic IBM Carbon.

## Palette

Burn the cyan-on-black launch look. It reads bootcamp, not laboratory.

- **bg-primary**: `#0A0B0E` (near-black, blue undertone — not pure #000, which feels cheap on OLED)
- **bg-elevated**: `#13151A` (cards, panels, code blocks)
- **bg-paper**: `#F6F5F1` (light-mode swatch sections; warm cream, never #FFFFFF)
- **fg-primary**: `#EDEBE6` on dark / `#0A0B0E` on cream
- **fg-secondary**: `#8B8A85` (mute, captions, byline)
- **border-hairline**: `#1F2128` on dark / `#D9D6CE` on cream — 1px, never thicker
- **accent-signal**: `#FF5B2E` (signal-orange — a controlled flare, descended from Anduril/Hermès, not Slack-orange)
- **accent-deep**: `#1B3A8B` (deep-archive blue, used sparingly for citations, hyperlinks, footnotes)
- **warn**: `#E8B341` (amber, used only for non-decorative warnings — vulnerability disclosures, edit-mode banners)
- **success-quiet**: `#3A6B4C` (forest, never neon green — this is a lab, not a CI dashboard)

Five colors do 90% of the work. Orange is the only flare. No gradients on the homepage. No glow effects. Color earns its place.

## Typography

Three families, all variable, all already loadable from a single subset.

- **Display / headings**: **GT Sectra** (or fallback **Source Serif 4 Variable**) — high-contrast transitional serif. This is the move. Every cyber and AI company uses geometric sans. A serif at scale reads "press release from a real institution" — Anthropic does it, Stripe Press does it, defense contractors do it. Tracking -0.02em at display sizes, -0.01em at H2.
- **Body / UI**: **Inter Variable** (already loaded) — kept for paragraph copy and chrome. 400/500/600 only.
- **Mono / code / specimens**: **JetBrains Mono Variable** — for code blocks, hex codes, file paths, the token tables. Mono is half the museum aesthetic.

Scale (modular, ratio 1.250 / major third):
`12 · 14 · 16 · 20 · 24 · 32 · 44 · 64 · 96 · 128`. Display headings start at 64 desktop, 44 mobile. Body is 16/24. Captions and specimen labels are 12 mono uppercase tracked +0.08em.

## Hero pattern (above the fold)

Three columns, asymmetric, no carousel, no animation on load.

- **Left column (40%)**: serif H1 across two lines — `AtomEons. / Independent AI laboratory.` H1 stands alone. Below it, a 14px Inter byline: `Marco Island, FL · est. 2026 · Solo research, full disclosure.` Below that, two text links underlined in `accent-deep`: `Read the papers →` and `See the work →`.
- **Center column (35%)**: a single live specimen — a code block in JetBrains Mono showing the current token export, scrolling slowly, with a hairline border and a 12px label `tokens.json — live`. It is the actual file. Not a mock.
- **Right column (25%)**: a vertical specimen strip — five color swatches stacked, each labeled with hex + role + WCAG contrast pair. Looks like a real Pantone book opened to a page.

No hero image. No video. No CTA button shouting "Get Started." The page is the product.

## Signature move

**The Token Ribbon.** A persistent 28px band fixed to the bottom of the viewport showing the *current design system snapshot*: `bg #0A0B0E · accent #FF5B2E · type GT Sectra 64/64 · grid 8px · build 2026.06.02`. It updates as you scroll through sections — when you enter the Cyber section, it shows that section's tokens. When you hit the Learn section, it changes. It is a status bar for the design system itself, and it tells you, every second, that you are inside a published spec. No one else does this. This alone gets us on Awwwards.

## Motion language

Motion is forbidden by default. It appears only in three places:

1. **The Token Ribbon** updates with a 200ms crossfade on section change.
2. **Hairline reveals** — section dividers draw left-to-right on scroll-into-view, 400ms, ease-out, once.
3. **Hover state on links** — underline thickens from 1px to 2px in 120ms. That is the entire vocabulary.

No parallax. No scroll-jacking. No "scroll to continue." No floating shapes. Motion is a deliberate authored event, not ambient noise.

## Navigation

Header is 56px, sticky, hairline bottom border, fully opaque (no blur — blur is 2022).

Left: `AtomEons` in GT Sectra 18px, no logo mark, no icon. The wordmark is the logo. Right: five items in Inter 14px, no caps: `research · cybersecurity · learn · build · about`. Active state is a 1px underline in `accent-signal`, nothing else. No background fill, no pill. On scroll past hero, header gains the Token Ribbon below it as a second row.

No hamburger on mobile. The five items collapse to a horizontal scroll strip. Hamburgers are an admission that the IA is bloated.

## Footer

One screen tall on desktop. Four columns of links in JetBrains Mono 12px uppercase. Above the columns: the full color palette as a 100% width row of swatches, each 1/8 of the screen wide, hex labels overlaid. Below the columns: a single line — `AtomEons Systems Laboratory · independent · Marco Island, FL · all source visible · ATOM-AESUITE-2026-0419` — followed by the Token Ribbon's last published export as a downloadable `tokens.json` link.

## Photography direction

Nano Banana Pro prompt template:

> "Editorial photograph, large-format, natural daylight from a north-facing window, single subject [SUBJECT — e.g., 'a matte-black server rack' / 'a stack of bound research papers' / 'a hand holding a USB security key'], shot on Mamiya 7 with Portra 400, shallow depth of field at f/4, subject offset to left third of frame, negative space right, warm cream background (#F6F5F1), no props, no people unless specified, no text overlay, no glow, no neon, no gradient, no cyber aesthetic clichés, museum-catalog composition, archival quality, 4:5 portrait or 3:2 landscape."

Every image looks like it belongs in MoMA's Department of Architecture and Design.

## Homepage outline

1. **Hero** — three-column specimen as above.
2. **Manifesto strip** — one sentence in 44px serif: `We publish what we discover, ship what we build, and disclose what we cannot yet verify.` Hairline above and below.
3. **The disciplines** — three cards: `Cybersecurity` / `AI Research` / `Open Education`. Each card is a swatch + serif title + 80-word abstract + signed-paper-style citation footnote.
4. **Specimen: the papers** — live grid of the latest 6 ÆoNs Research papers, each rendered as a museum object card with title, date, SHA-256, page count, abstract first line.
5. **Specimen: the products** — OrangeBox and B00KMakor shown as plate-photographs (Nano Banana Pro output), not screenshots. Underneath each, a small mono-text price tag and a `purchase →` link in `accent-deep`.
6. **The curriculum** — 67 cyber and learn images become the specimen here. A grid wall, 12 columns, no captions on hover, click reveals the full lesson page.
7. **Build log** — last 10 commits to public repos, mono, timestamped, linked. Proof of life.
8. **Colophon / footer** — the palette swatch row + four columns + Token Ribbon archive.

## Why this wins on all three identities

**Cybersecurity gravitas**: Defense and intelligence interfaces are *quiet*. They are mono, hairline-ruled, color-restricted, no animation. The visual vocabulary of this site is the vocabulary of a SCIF terminal, not a startup landing page. Buyers in that world recognize it on contact. The serif on top of mono on top of unapologetic negative space says "this is not a toy."

**AI college alternative**: A museum and a university share a vocabulary — specimens, catalogs, citations, archives, plates, footnotes. The homepage already *looks* like a syllabus reading list. The 67-image grid becomes a course catalog without ever saying "course catalog." We are not pretending to be a college. We are building something that has the bearing of one.

**Design inspiration**: Nobody is doing this. The current peer set (Vercel, Linear, Anthropic, Stripe) all converge on the same Inter-on-black geometric-sans aesthetic. A high-contrast serif + mono specimen-book treatment with a live Token Ribbon at the bottom is a distinct signature on first frame. Awwwards-curators reward signature. Designers will screenshot the Token Ribbon. That is the share-vector.

## Honest concessions

- **GT Sectra is paid** (Grilli Type, ~$700 single-style desktop, more for web). Source Serif 4 Variable is a credible free fallback but loses ~15% of the gravitas. Budget call required.
- **A serif-led hero is a commercial risk on mobile.** At 360px viewport, 44px GT Sectra wraps awkwardly; the three-column hero collapses to single-column and loses some of its "specimen page" character. Mobile will look more like a magazine cover than a museum plate. Acceptable trade.
- **The Token Ribbon is engineering work** — it needs to actually read from a live tokens file and update per section. ~2 days to build correctly. If it ships static, it is just decoration and the entire concept weakens.
- **Light-mode swatch sections + dark-mode hero = two themes on one page.** This is harder than it sounds. Section transitions need to be deliberate, not jarring. Test on a 4K monitor and a 6.1" phone before shipping.
- **No hero image / no video** will read to some stakeholders as "underdone." It is not — it is restraint — but expect pushback from anyone whose mental model of "premium site" is Apple's product launches. We are Stripe Press, not iPhone.com. Hold the line.
- **The Build Log section requires real commit activity.** If commits go quiet for a week, the section reads as abandoned. This is a feature (forces shipping) but also a risk.
- **The serif/mono/Inter trio is three font families.** Heavier than ideal. Variable formats and subsetting keep it under 90KB total, but it is real weight. The aesthetic earns it.

# NOIR-CINEMA — Design Direction for AtomEons Homepage

## The thesis

The current site (black + bio-cyan + amber) reads "startup with a good logo." It does not read "$1T cybersecurity research laboratory that also happens to teach you AI better than Stanford." NOIR-CINEMA fixes this by treating the homepage as a film, not a landing page. Full-bleed, oversized, restrained color, restrained motion, one signature move that nobody else owns.

The closest spiritual references: Anduril's product pages (military gravitas via dim photography and aerospace-grade type), Apple's Vision Pro launch (full-bleed cinematic with sparse copy), DeepMind's research site (academic restraint), and the editorial pages of The New York Times Magazine (oversized photography paired with serif body copy that says "this is serious"). Burn the cyan-amber palette. Keep the bio-cyan only as the single accent for *live signal* — terminal cursor, build counter, status dot.

## Palette

- **Background:** `#08090B` (near-black, slight warm shift — not pure `#000000`, which reads cheap on OLED)
- **Surface 1 (cards, panels):** `#0F1114`
- **Surface 2 (elevated):** `#161A1F`
- **Border (hairline):** `#1F242B`
- **Foreground primary:** `#F4F4F2` (off-white, paper-tone — not pure white)
- **Foreground secondary:** `#9CA3AF`
- **Foreground tertiary (meta, timestamps):** `#5A6068`
- **Accent — singular, used sparingly:** `#22F0D5` (the existing bio-cyan, surviving as the *only* color on the page)
- **Warn / live / recording:** `#FF4D4D` (BBC News red — used exclusively for the live build counter dot and "LIVE" markers)
- **No gradients. No glass. No glow. Color appears at most twice per viewport.**

## Typography

- **Display:** *Söhne Breit* or *GT America Mono Extended* if budget allows; otherwise **Inter Display Variable** at weight 900, tracking `-0.04em`, leading `0.92`. Set hero type at clamp(80px, 12vw, 200px). Variable-weight axis used for one trick (see signature move).
- **Body:** *Söhne* or **Inter Variable** at weight 400, 17px on desktop, leading 1.55. Tracking 0.
- **Editorial body (long-form / About / Research):** **Newsreader** (serif) at 19px, leading 1.6. This is the move that signals "academic depth, not landing-page copy."
- **Mono:** **Berkeley Mono** ($75 license — pay it) or **JetBrains Mono Variable** as the fallback. Used for code, build numbers, timestamps, hashes, file paths. Never for headlines.
- **Scale:** modular ratio 1.333. Sizes: 12 / 14 / 17 / 22 / 30 / 40 / 56 / 80 / 120 / 200. No type sits between those values. Whitespace does the work that smaller jumps usually do.

## Hero pattern (above the fold)

Full-bleed `100vh` cinematic photograph from `/cyber-images/`. Image runs edge to edge with a `linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(8,9,11,0) 40%, rgba(8,9,11,0.9) 100%)` overlay so text rides the bottom third. Photograph stays still — no Ken Burns. The page does not move when you arrive. Stillness is the statement.

Bottom-left, two lines:
- Eyebrow mono: `ÆONS RESEARCH LABORATORY · MARCO ISLAND, FL · EST. 2024`
- Headline display: a single sentence, no period, set in 120px+. Example: **"We teach machines to behave"** or **"The laboratory between you and the model"**.

Bottom-right, hairline-bordered live signal panel: build counter, last commit hash, current pacific time, FOUNDER_SALARY install count. Mono. Red dot pulsing at 1.2s. This is the cybersecurity signal — the site is *operating*, not displaying.

No CTA button above the fold. No "Get started." The fold is for stating who we are.

## Signature move — *The Variable-Weight Reveal*

As the user scrolls past the hero, the display headline below it begins as weight 100 (hairline) and animates to weight 900 (black) over the first 400px of scroll, while letter-spacing tightens from `0.04em` to `-0.04em`. The headline doesn't move. It *thickens*. Like a thought solidifying. This works because Inter Variable supports the full weight axis natively — it's one font file, no swapping. Nobody else does this. Stripe doesn't. Vercel doesn't. Linear doesn't. This is the screenshot that gets posted to /r/web_design.

## Motion language

Motion appears in three places, never more:

1. The variable-weight reveal (above)
2. The live build counter pulse (red dot, 1.2s)
3. Section transitions — sticky-scroll where the headline pins while the photograph below it changes (one image dissolves to the next at 600ms, ease-in-out)

No parallax. No floating cards. No hover-tilts. No scroll-triggered confetti. The site is calm. The site is a research laboratory.

## Navigation

Header is a single horizontal hairline row at `64px` tall, `#1F242B` bottom border. Logo left (just "ÆONS" in display weight 700, 18px). Five items center-right: **Research · Learn · Build · About · Engage**. Mono, 12px, uppercase, tracking 0.08em. Active state is a 1px underline at offset 4px, no color change. On hover, the inactive items dim to `#5A6068`. No mega-menus. No dropdowns. If a section has children, the page itself handles them.

## Footer

Tall — 60vh — and treated as its own composition. Left half: oversized display word "ÆONS" set in 240px weight 900, hairline border at bottom. Right half: four columns of mono links (Research / Learn / Build / Lab), each capped at 6 items. Bottom hairline row: copyright, current commit hash, "Marco Island, FL · 25°56'N 81°43'W" in mono. No social icons. No newsletter signup (that lives on /engage). The footer is a closing shot, not a sitemap dump.

## Photography direction — Nano Banana Pro prompt template

> Cinematic 35mm photograph, shot on Kodak Vision3 500T, slight grain, anamorphic 2.39:1 letterbox crop. Subject: [SUBJECT]. Lighting: single hard key from camera-left, deep shadow side, ambient fill from a cold blue monitor glow at 5600K reading 6 stops under key. Color grade: desaturated cyan in shadows, warm tungsten in highlights, blacks crushed to `#08090B`, no pure white anywhere — peak luminance `#E8E5E0`. Composition: subject occupies left or right third, vast negative space, horizon line lowered to bottom 30%. No people unless requested. No text overlays. No lens flares. Mood: 2:47 AM in a research facility. References: Roger Deakins lighting, *Blade Runner 2049* color science, Anduril product photography, *The Insider* (1999) color grade.

Subject library to generate: server racks in low light, hands on a mechanical keyboard, a single CRT monitor in darkness, a circuit board macro, a building exterior at blue hour, a chalkboard with equations, an empty lecture hall, an open laptop on a wooden desk with one warm lamp.

## Homepage outline (6 sections, in order)

1. **Hero** — full-bleed photograph + live signal panel (above the fold)
2. **The Thesis** — Variable-weight reveal headline + 60-word paragraph in Newsreader serif. *"AtomEons is a research laboratory. We ship runtime cognition, frontier security tooling, and a free AI education better than most universities. We are headquartered in Marco Island, Florida, and we operate alone."*
3. **Three doors** — three full-bleed cinematic photographs stacked vertically with sticky display labels: **Cyber** / **Learn** / **Build**. Each is its own scroll moment, ~100vh tall. Clicking enters the section.
4. **Live receipts** — full-width strip on `#0F1114` showing real numbers in oversized mono: install count, lines of code shipped this week, papers published, commits, current MRR. Updated from API, no fake metrics. This is the cyber credibility moment.
5. **Research strip** — three latest papers as editorial cards (serif title, mono date, 40-word abstract). Linked to `/research/<id>`. This is the AI-college credibility moment.
6. **Founder note** — one full-bleed photograph (operator's actual hand or workspace), one paragraph signed "— Atom McCree, Marco Island." Set in Newsreader, max 80 words. This is the design-inspiration moment — most companies don't end the homepage on a signed letter.

## Why this wins on all three identities at once

**Cybersecurity gravitas:** the palette, the live signal panel with red dot, the mono timestamps, the desaturated photography, the absence of marketing copy above the fold — all of it reads like Anduril, Palantir, or a NIST publication. We don't *claim* to be cyber; we *look* cyber. Defense-industrial design language is the most underused vocabulary on the consumer web, and it is exactly the right vocabulary for a laboratory.

**AI college alternative:** Newsreader serif for long-form, editorial card density on the research strip, signed founder note, the word "laboratory" everywhere, no growth-hack copy. The site reads like the front page of a small private research institute that publishes papers — because that is what we are. The free-education claim becomes credible because the surface itself is academic.

**Design-inspiration site:** the variable-weight reveal is the signature move. It is one trick, executed perfectly, with technical novelty (real variable-font axis animation, not CSS faux-bold). It is screenshot-able. It is GIF-able. It is the thing people will post when they share the site. Combined with the cinematic photography library and the stillness-as-statement hero, the site has the *one* unmistakable visual signature that Awwwards looks for, without resorting to WebGL noise or particle systems that age in 18 months.

## Honest concessions

- **Berkeley Mono costs money.** $75 personal, $187 commercial. Worth it. JetBrains Mono Variable is the free fallback but reads more "engineering blog" than "research laboratory."
- **The variable-weight reveal will not work in browsers older than Chrome 88 / Safari 14.** That's 0.4% of traffic. Acceptable.
- **Full-bleed cinematic photography on every interior page is bandwidth-expensive.** We need to commit to Vercel Image Optimization with AVIF + responsive srcset, and we need a real budget for hero images (Nano Banana Pro generations at scale, then human-curated). Otherwise the photography quality drops and the whole illusion collapses. Photography is not optional in this direction — it is the load-bearing element.
- **Calm doesn't sell to everyone.** Some visitors arrive expecting "click here to start" energy and will bounce. We are betting that the people we *want* (researchers, enterprise security buyers, serious learners) read calm as competence, while the bounce-rate population was never going to pay $9,999 for Skilski Verify anyway.
- **The variable-weight reveal can be copied within 6 months.** That's fine. By the time it's copied, we've moved on to the next signature move. The goal isn't to own the technique forever — it's to be the first site that did it well, and to use the resulting attention to ship the laboratory.
- **No gradient, no glass, no glow is a discipline, not a constraint.** Every PR will want to add a glow. Every contributor will want a hover-tilt. The release-steward role exists to say no. This is the part that requires governance, not design.

# POST-FRONTIER

A design direction for AtomEons, by AtomEons Systems Laboratory. Marco Island, FL. 2026-06-02.

---

## Premise

The current frontier aesthetic — black canvas, neon cyan, glassy haze, perpetual countdown timers — was the language of 2022-2025 AI. Every lab now speaks it. Anthropic dimmed theirs. OpenAI is still mid-rebrand. xAI shipped a black hole. Mistral is doing watercolors. The signal-to-noise on dark mode AI sites is now zero. Going darker means joining a crowd.

POST-FRONTIER is the opposite move. The page is white. The page is calm. The page is a laboratory under fluorescent light at 2pm on a Tuesday, not a war room at 3am. The accent is not cyan. The motion is not constant. The work is the show, not the chrome.

This wins because it reads as "we already won the frontier; now we run the institution."

---

## Palette

- **Background:** `#FAFAF7` — paper-white with 2% warm cream. Not pure white (`#FFFFFF` reads sterile and dates to 2010 web). Not bone (`#F5F5F0` reads vintage). This is exam-booklet white.
- **Foreground:** `#0A0A0A` — near-black with no blue tint. Reads as ink, not screen.
- **Border:** `#E5E5E0` — one shade off bg. Visible at 1px, invisible at distance. Anti-shadow.
- **Accent:** `#CCFF00` — electric lime. Brutalist signal color. Reads as radiation badge, hi-vis vest, server LED. Used in <2% of pixels on any given screen. Hot pink and chartreuse were considered; lime wins because cyber-defense surplus / safety-gear semantics map directly to "on the front lines."
- **Warn:** `#FF3B00` — saturated red-orange. For incident states, expired surfaces, security advisories. Never decorative.
- **Subtle:** `#737370` — secondary text, metadata, timestamps.

No gradients. No glassmorphism. No glow. The whole site is two colors plus a punch.

---

## Typography

- **Heading:** **Söhne Breit** or **NB Architekt** (paid) — wide brutalist geometric sans, slightly condensed at large sizes, full caps for display. Free fallback: **Space Grotesk** at 700 weight with `letter-spacing: -0.04em` and `font-stretch: 110%`. Display sizes go 96/72/56/40/32 px, never in between.
- **Body:** Keep **Inter Variable**, already loaded. Set `font-variation-settings: "opsz" 28` for headings, `"opsz" 14` for body. Body copy at 16/24 with `font-feature-settings: "ss01", "cv11"` for the open-aperture variant. This is what Stripe, Linear, and Vercel use, and the cv11 alternates make it ours.
- **Mono:** **Berkeley Mono** (paid, $75) or **JetBrains Mono** (free fallback). Used for receipt numbers, SHA hashes, timestamps, code, navigation timecodes.
- **Scale rule:** 4-step modular scale at 1.5 ratio anchored on 16px body. Nothing exists between steps. Display:96, h1:64, h2:48, h3:32, h4:24, body:16, small:13, micro:11. The grid is the rule; the rule is the grid.

---

## Hero pattern

Above the fold, three things only:

1. **A timecode** in mono, top-left: `2026.06.02 / MARCO ISLAND, FL / 14:07 EDT`. Live ticking. This is the receipt that we exist in time.
2. **A wide brutalist headline** taking 60% of viewport width, full-bleed left, no centering: `WE BUILD THE LAB. WE TEACH THE LAB. WE DEFEND THE LAB.` Three sentences, three weights — first line `font-weight: 900`, second `700`, third `900`. Lime period at the end of each.
3. **A single artifact pane** to the right or below — a live SHA, a live receipt, a live security advisory, a live student count. One thing, mono, lime accent on the live number.

No carousel. No video. No 3D. No countdown. The hero is a statement, not a stage.

---

## Signature move

**The Receipt Strip.**

Every page has a 24px-tall mono strip pinned to the bottom of the viewport (not the page) showing the last 5 verifiable receipts from the site itself, scrolling at human-reading speed (one item every 8 seconds, no faster). Each receipt is a real timestamped event: a SHA emission, a student enrollment, a security audit pass, a paper preregistration. Click any receipt to jump to its evidence page.

The Receipt Strip is the "we are real, we are operating right now, every claim has proof" move that no other AI lab is doing. Anthropic has a blog. OpenAI has a changelog. Anduril has nothing public. We have a live tape, the way the NYSE has a ticker.

This is the screenshot people share.

---

## Motion language

Motion appears in exactly four places and nowhere else:

1. **Receipt Strip** — slow horizontal scroll, never paused, never accelerated.
2. **Hero timecode** — second hand updates once per second, no easing.
3. **Hover state on receipts and nav items** — 80ms underline reveal, lime, no fade, no transform.
4. **Page transitions** — 120ms cross-fade, no slide, no scale.

No parallax. No scroll-jacking. No reveal-on-scroll. No Lenis. No mouse-tracking gradients. The page does not perform for the visitor. The visitor performs by reading.

This is the Anduril / Anthropic move. Stillness is the flex.

---

## Navigation

Header is a single 56px-tall row, pinned, no shadow, 1px lime bottom border on scroll.

Left: `AE` monogram in lime, brutalist, 24px. Click returns home.

Center-left: five plain text items, mono, 13px, uppercase, tracked: `LAB · LEARN · DEFEND · RECEIPTS · OPERATOR`. No "Products." No "Solutions." No "Resources." The five words map to what we are: research, education, cybersecurity, evidence, the human.

Right: a single primary action, lime-filled black-text rectangle, brutalist: `ENROLL — FREE`.

Active state: 2px lime underline, no animation, no fade. You are here. That is the only state.

---

## Footer

One 320px-tall block. White-on-black inversion — the only dark surface on the entire site. Mono everywhere. Four columns: `LAB` (papers, receipts, security audits), `LEARN` (skills, courses, syllabus, enrollment), `OPERATE` (orangebox, b00kmakor, real revenue products), `HUMAN` (operator, contact, lab address — Marco Island FL with real lat/long).

Below the columns: the SHA-256 of the current commit, the build timestamp, the Vercel deploy ID. All real. All clickable to the receipt that proves them.

No social icons. No newsletter form. No "made with love." The footer is the colophon of a research journal.

---

## Photography direction (Nano Banana Pro)

We do not use stock photos, hero illustrations, or AI-generated humans. We use one of three image types only:

1. **Whitebox architectural renders** — clean isometric or 3/4 perspective of physical objects: a server rack, a lab bench, an OrangeBox unit, a stack of papers, a key on a desk. Lit flat, no harsh shadows, paper-white background bleeds into image background.
2. **Document scans** — real preregistration PDFs, real security advisories, real lab notes, photographed flat on a scanner with a 2% grain.
3. **Studio diagrams** — flat 2D technical drawings: network topology, lattice structure, course-flow graph, drawn in 1px `#0A0A0A` strokes on `#FAFAF7` with lime accent for the active node.

**Nano Banana Pro prompt template:**

> Studio product photography on paper-white background `#FAFAF7`, soft north-facing window light from upper left at 11am, no harsh shadows, no contact shadow, subject is `[OBJECT]` rendered at `[SCALE]` filling 40% of frame center-weighted, single lime `#CCFF00` accent element on subject (`[ACCENT_DETAIL]`), shot on Phase One IQ4, 80mm lens, f/8, ISO 100, color profile: muted, near-black ink-tone shadows, no chromatic aberration, no bokeh, no film grain, no vignette, sharp throughout, isometric or 3/4 perspective, technical-catalog aesthetic, MUJI meets Anduril meets a 1970s Braun catalog, no humans, no text, no logos, no brand marks.

The lime accent is the only color that ever appears in our imagery. Everything else is paper, ink, and the object.

---

## Homepage outline

1. **Hero** — timecode, three-line statement, one live artifact.
2. **The Three Identities** — three full-width rows, each 50/50 split. Row 1: cybersecurity (the lab as defense). Row 2: AI college (the lab as institution). Row 3: design inspiration (the lab as artifact). Each row: brutalist headline left, one Nano Banana Pro image right, one mono receipt link below the image.
3. **Live receipt grid** — 12 latest verifiable events from across the system in a 4x3 grid. Each card 1px border, mono timestamp, brutalist event type, 13px plain-text description, lime SHA-link to evidence.
4. **The Curriculum** — full-width text block listing the current 721 skills as a 4-column dense mono list, with the 36 completed ones rendered in `#0A0A0A` and the rest in `#737370`. The honesty move. Visitors see what's done and what isn't.
5. **The Defense Posture** — single full-width section, dark inversion (`#0A0A0A` background, `#FAFAF7` text), one paragraph on what AtomEons defends and how, with three security advisory links below in mono.
6. **Real revenue products** — two-card row, `/orangebox` and `/b00kmakor`, brutalist treatment, real pricing in mono, no marketing copy, one sentence each.
7. **The Operator** — single section, Atom's name in display type, one paragraph of plain biography, one photograph (document-scan treatment, not portrait), the lab address with real lat/long.
8. **Footer** — as specified above.

The Receipt Strip pins to the bottom of the viewport across all sections.

---

## Why this wins on all three identities

**Cybersecurity:** Brutalist geometry, hi-vis lime, real-time receipts, security advisory links in the footer and on the homepage, and the document-scan photography read as defense-industrial. We are not "AI safety" theater. We look like a contractor with a clearance.

**AI college better than school:** Paper-white background, modular type scale, dense mono curriculum list, real syllabus visible on the homepage, no upsells, no testimonials, no "join the waitlist." The site reads as a printed course catalog from a real institution. The 36-of-721 honesty move is the trust signal no other AI school is making.

**Design inspiration / Awwwards:** The Receipt Strip is the signature move. Nobody else has it. It's animated, but functional. It's brutalist, but useful. It's the screenshot. The white-canvas-with-lime-accent palette is the antithesis of every dark-mode AI site shipping in 2026 — which means it photographs differently, archives differently, and gets remembered.

---

## Honest concessions

1. **The lime accent will polarize.** Some visitors will read it as construction-site, not couture. The fix is restraint — keep it under 2% of pixels and it reads as instrument, not graffiti.
